transcript: They fell in love with its lifelike figures, dramatic scene, and pristine white surface. But sculptures like this weren't meant to be white. Their paint had just faded after being buried or left out in the open air for hundreds of years. So when the Renaissance artists set out to imitate them, they left their masterpieces bare too. And that style took over, inspiring generations of sculptors. White marble became the norm. Along the way, art historians reinforced this bias.
